source: Learning Ansible with Rocky - Ansible Basics: Concepts and vocabulary

questions.q: Ansible is described as "agentless". What does that mean?
options: No software has to be deployed on the managed clients
A daemon must run on every managed client
It can only manage the control node itself
It needs a database agent to store host state
questions.why: Ansible is agentless: nothing is installed on the managed servers; it connects over SSH (or WinRM).

questions.q: Ansible is "idempotent". What does that guarantee?
options: Running the same play repeatedly converges to the same end state
Each run undoes what the previous run did
It runs faster on every subsequent execution
Tasks are executed in a random order
questions.why: Idempotent means the same effect each time it is run; a task already in the desired state makes no change.

questions.q: Which protocol does Ansible use by default to configure Linux clients?
options: SSH
WinRM
HTTP
SNMP
questions.why: SSH for Linux, WinRM for Windows; an API can be used when neither is available.

questions.q: Ansible is mainly push-based and "stateless". What does that imply?
options: It keeps no state between runs and re-checks state on each execution
It stores every host's state in a central database
Clients push their state to the server on a timer
It cannot detect configuration drift at all
questions.why: Being push-based it does not keep the state of targets between runs; it performs new state checks each time.

questions.q: In Ansible vocabulary, what is the "inventory"?
options: A file containing information about the managed servers
The list of installed modules
The facts gathered from a host
A playbook's list of tasks
questions.why: The inventory is a file describing the managed servers (hosts) and groups of hosts.

options: An abstraction of a task, such as creating a user or installing a package
A group of hosts
A YAML inventory file
A stored SSH key
questions.why: A module abstracts a task; Ansible ships many of them (more than 750).

options: A YAML file defining target hosts and the tasks to perform
A single shell command
A bundle of modules
An SSH configuration file
questions.why: A playbook is a YAML file defining the target servers and the tasks to run.

options: Global variables holding system information (hostname, OS version, network config...)
Handlers that restart services
The return codes of a playbook
Encrypted secrets stored in a vault
questions.why: Facts are variables about the system, gathered from the managed host.

options: Stopping or restarting a service in reaction to a change
Declaring the inventory
Gathering facts
Storing variables
questions.why: Handlers cause a service to be stopped or restarted when a change is notified.
